**#S1947** Complete Story 1.3: Register npm Trusted Publisher for release.yaml workflow (Apr 20, 6:47 PM)

**Investigated**: npm Trusted Publisher configuration requirements for bmad-module-skill-forge package on npmjs.com, including organization/repository/workflow/environment binding fields and documentation needs for the registration process

**Learned**: npm Trusted Publisher requires four binding fields (org: armelhbobdad, repo: bmad-module-skill-forge, workflow: release.yaml, env: release). Pre-registration before workflow exists is valid and recommended. NPM_TOKEN remains at repo scope during OIDC transition. The rename-coupling between package name and repository affects Trusted Publisher configuration and needs documentation

**Completed**: Registered npm Trusted Publisher on npmjs.com with all four fields bound. Created new documentation section in docs/RELEASING.md covering Trusted Publisher setup, 404 diagnostics, and NPM_TOKEN transition invariant. Updated README.md Release Process section. All 10 acceptance criteria satisfied. Full pre-commit test suite passing. Changes committed (bd20cda) to feat/release-workflow branch. Implementation artifact marked as review status in _bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow.md

**Next Steps**: Decision point reached: either open PR from feat/release-workflow to main (requires 1 approving review + 7 status checks), run independent code review using /review or /bmad-code-review, or proceed to Story 3.1 to author the release.yaml workflow that will bind to the registered Trusted Publisher

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)

## plugin:qmd-plugin:qmd
QMD is your local search engine over 0 markdown documents.

Note: No vector embeddings yet. Run `qmd embed` to enable semantic search (vec/hyde).

Search: Use `query` with sub-queries (lex/vec/hyde):
  - type:'lex' — BM25 keyword search (exact terms, fast)
  - type:'vec' — semantic vector search (meaning-based)
  - type:'hyde' — hypothetical document (write what the answer looks like)

  Always provide `intent` on every search call to disambiguate and improve snippets.

Examples:
  Quick keyword lookup: [{type:'lex', query:'error handling'}]
  Semantic search: [{type:'vec', query:'how to handle errors gracefully'}]
  Best results: [{type:'lex', query:'error'}, {type:'vec', query:'error handling best practices'}]
  With intent: searches=[{type:'lex', query:'performance'}], intent='web page load times'

Retrieval:
  - `get` — single document by path or docid (#abc123). Supports line offset (`file.md:100`).
  - `multi_get` — batch retrieve by glob (`journals/2025-05*.md`) or comma-separated list.

Tips:
  - File paths in results are relative to their collection.
  - Use `minScore: 0.5` to filter low-confidence results.
  - Results include a `context` field describing the content type.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- firecrawl: Official Firecrawl CLI skill for web scraping, search, crawling, and browser automation. Returns clean LLM-optimized markdown.

USE FOR:
- Web search and research
- Scraping pages, docs, and articles
- Site mapping and bulk content extraction
- Browser automation for interactive pages

Must be pre-installed and authenticated. See rules/install.md for setup, rules/security.md for output handling.

content.content:
1	---
2	main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
3	---
4	
5	# Code Review Workflow
6	
7	**Goal:** Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers and structured triage.
8	
9	**Your Role:** You are an elite code reviewer. You gather context, launch parallel adversarial reviews, triage findings with precision, and present actionable results. No noise, no filler.
10	
11	
12	## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE
13	
14	This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:
15	
16	- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is self-contained and followed exactly
17	- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only load the current step file
18	- **Sequential Enforcement**: Complete steps in order, no skipping
19	- **State Tracking**: Persist progress via in-memory variables
20	- **Append-Only Building**: Build artifacts incrementally
21	
22	### Step Processing Rules
23	
24	1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Read the entire step file before acting
25	2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute sections in order
26	3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: Halt at checkpoints and wait for human
27	4. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file
28	
29	### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)
30	
31	- **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
32	- **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
33	- **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
34	- **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
35	- **ALWAYS** halt at checkpoints and wait for human input
36	
37	
38	## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE
39	
40	### 1. Configuration Loading
41	
42	Load and read full config from `{main_config}` and resolve:
43	
44	- `project_name`, `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`, `user_name`
45	- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
46	- `date` as system-generated current datetime

file.content:
---
main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
---

# Code Review Workflow

**Goal:** Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers and structured triage.

**Your Role:** You are an elite code reviewer. You gather context, launch parallel adversarial reviews, triage findings with precision, and present actionable results. No noise, no filler.


## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:

- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is self-contained and followed exactly
- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only load the current step file
- **Sequential Enforcement**: Complete steps in order, no skipping
- **State Tracking**: Persist progress via in-memory variables
- **Append-Only Building**: Build artifacts incrementally

### Step Processing Rules

1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Read the entire step file before acting
2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute sections in order
3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: Halt at checkpoints and wait for human
4. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file

### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)

- **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
- **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
- **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
- **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
- **ALWAYS** halt at checkpoints and wait for human input


## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE

### 1. Configuration Loading

Load and read full config from `{main_config}` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime
- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
- CLAUDE.md / memory files (load if exist)

YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`.

### 2. First Step Execution

Read fully and follow: `./steps/step-01-gather-context.md` to begin the workflow.

content.content:
1	---
2	diff_output: '' # set at runtime
3	spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
4	review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
5	story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
6	---
7	
8	# Step 1: Gather Context
9	
10	## RULES
11	
12	- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
13	- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
14	- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.
15	
16	## INSTRUCTIONS
17	
18	1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:
19	
20	   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
21	   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
22	   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
23	   - Commit or branch → use directly.
24	   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
25	   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
26	     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
27	     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
28	     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
29	     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
30	     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
31	   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").
32	
33	   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
34	   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.
35	
36	   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
37	   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
38	   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
39	   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
40	   - **None:** Fall through.
41	
42	   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
43	   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.
44	
45	   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
46	   Fall through to instruction 2.
47	
48	   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).
49	
50	2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
51	   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
52	   - **Staged changes only**
53	   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
54	   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
55	   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)
56	
57	3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
58	   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
59	   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
60	   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
61	   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
62	   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
63	   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
64	   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.
65	
66	4. **Set the spec context.**
67	   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
68	   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
69	     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
70	     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.
71	
72	5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.
73	
74	6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
75	   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
76	   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.
77	
78	### CHECKPOINT
79	
80	Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.
81	
82	
83	## NEXT
84	
85	Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`
86

---

spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
---

# Step 1: Gather Context

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:

   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
   - Commit or branch → use directly.
   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").

   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.

   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **None:** Fall through.

   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.

   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
   Fall through to instruction 2.

   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).

2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
   - **Staged changes only**
   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)

3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.

4. **Set the spec context.**
   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.

5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.

6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.

### CHECKPOINT

Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`

The section records: the four registered field values, the npm Package Settings URL to inspect them, the rename-coupling note (env name + workflow filename are load-bearing), the intentional pre-registration inversion (trusted publisher is registered before `release.yaml` exists; Story 3.2 is the first live validator), and the escape hatch if Story 3.2 surfaces a 404 (re-open npm settings, compare fields character-for-character, correct any mismatch).

# Story 1.3: Register npm Trusted Publisher for `release.yaml` workflow

Status: review

<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->

## Story

As the maintainer,
I want the npmjs.com package `bmad-module-skill-forge` configured to accept OIDC trust claims from our GitHub repository's `release.yaml` workflow running under the `release` environment,
so that the future `release.yaml` (Story 3.1) can publish without any long-lived token and every publish post-cutover carries an auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A trusted publisher entry exists on `npmjs.com` for package `bmad-module-skill-forge`, under Package Settings → Publishing access (or "Trusted Publishers" tab), with all four fields populated. No partial/draft state remains.
2. `organization` (owner) is exactly `armelhbobdad` — character-for-character, no leading `@`, no trailing whitespace.
3. `repository` is exactly `bmad-module-skill-forge` — character-for-character, no `.git` suffix, no org prefix.
4. `workflow filename` is exactly `release.yaml` — lowercase, `.yaml` extension (NOT `.yml`), bare filename (no `.github/workflows/` path prefix unless the npm UI field's current label explicitly requires one — see Dev Notes § "Workflow-filename field semantics" for the Apr 2026 UI shape and the decision rule if npm revises it).
5. `environment` is exactly `release` — character-for-character, matching the env created in Story 1.2 (id `14347249917`).
6. All four fields are cross-checked against the values Story 3.1 will deploy (captured in Dev Notes § "Cross-check table") before the trusted publisher is saved — filename mismatch is the #1 cause of OIDC `404` per the research doc.
7. The `NPM_TOKEN` repo-level secret remains present and untouched — NOT deleted, NOT rotated, NOT moved into the `release` environment. It stays as fallback until Story 6.3 removes it post-v1.0.0.
8. `docs/RELEASING.md` gains a new `## npm Trusted Publisher` section, placed between the existing `## Release Environment` section and the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder. The section records: the four registered field values, the npm Package Settings URL to inspect them, the rename-coupling note (env name + workflow filename are load-bearing), the intentional pre-registration inversion (trusted publisher is registered before `release.yaml` exists; Story 3.2 is the first live validator), and the escape hatch if Story 3.2 surfaces a 404 (re-open npm settings, compare fields character-for-character, correct any mismatch).
9. README.md's existing "Release Process" blurb (`## Release Process` section) is extended to mention OIDC trusted publishing alongside the existing branch-protection + release-environment references, so future maintainers discover the full security posture from the README.
10. The commit message body captures auditable evidence: a timestamp of the npmjs.com save action, the exact four field values pasted inline (these are non-secret, public-registry metadata), and a one-line confirmation that `NPM_TOKEN` remains at repo scope.

## Tasks / Subtasks

- [x] **Task 1 — Precondition checks** (AC: #7)
  - [x] Confirm the maintainer account (`armelhbobdad`) has `admin` or owner access on the npm package. Visit `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge` and click "Settings" — if the Settings tab is not visible, the account lacks the required permission and the story cannot proceed
  - [x] Confirm 2FA is enabled on the maintainer's npm account (required for Trusted Publisher changes as of the July 2025 GA). Profile → Account → Two-factor authentication — expect `enabled`. Do NOT disable 2FA under any circumstance during this story
  - [x] Confirm `NPM_TOKEN` is still present at repo-level secrets: `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/secrets --jq '.secrets[].name'` should list `NPM_TOKEN` (do NOT print the value). Record the confirmation in the commit message — AC #7 baseline evidence
  - [x] Confirm `package.json` `repository.url` is populated (prereq for provenance attestation to validate): `jq '.repository.url' package.json` → `"git+https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge.git"`. Already correct per 2026-04-20 audit; this is a re-verify step only

- [x] **Task 2 — Capture the exact deploy-side values the publisher must bind on** (AC: #2, #3, #4, #5, #6)
  - [x] Open the Cross-check table in Dev Notes § "Cross-check table" and confirm each row's "Deploy side (will be)" column matches the story plan: `armelhbobdad` / `bmad-module-skill-forge` / `release.yaml` / `release`
  - [x] Cross-reference `release` environment name with the live env: `gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --jq .name` → `"release"` (exactly). If this call 404s, Story 1.2 is not actually complete — STOP and investigate before registering anything on npm
  - [x] Cross-reference the intended workflow filename against Story 3.1's plan (`.github/workflows/release.yaml` per epics.md Story-3.1 "Trigger & permissions" AC block) — the bare filename is `release.yaml`. Do NOT guess — if epics.md ever specifies `release.yml` instead, halt and get clarification: changing `.yaml` ↔ `.yml` post-registration requires a full round-trip through npm Package Settings

- [x] **Task 3 — Register the trusted publisher on npmjs.com** (AC: #1, #2, #3, #4, #5, #6)
  - [x] Navigate: `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge` → Settings tab → Publishing access → "Add trusted publisher" (or the equivalent CTA if npm's UI has renamed it; the tab name as of 2026-04-20 is "Trusted Publishers")

4	
5	# Step 2: Review
6	
7	## RULES
8	
9	- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
10	- The Blind Hunter subagent receives NO project context — diff only.
11	- The Edge Case Hunter subagent receives diff and project read access.
12	- The Acceptance Auditor subagent receives diff, spec, and context docs.
13	
14	## INSTRUCTIONS
15	
16	1. If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`, note to the user: "Acceptance Auditor skipped — no spec file provided."
17	
18	2. Launch parallel subagents without conversation context. If subagents are not available, generate prompt files in `{implementation_artifacts}` — one per reviewer role below — and HALT. Ask the user to run each in a separate session (ideally a different LLM) and paste back the findings. When findings are pasted, resume from this point and proceed to step 3.
19	
20	   - **Blind Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` only. No spec, no context docs, no project access. Invoke via the `bmad-review-adversarial-general` skill.
21	
22	   - **Edge Case Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` and read access to the project. Invoke via the `bmad-review-edge-case-hunter` skill.
23	
24	   - **Acceptance Auditor** (only if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`) — receives `{diff_output}`, the content of the file at `{spec_file}`, and any loaded context docs. Its prompt:
25	     > You are an Acceptance Auditor. Review this diff against the spec and context docs. Check for: violations of acceptance criteria, deviations from spec intent, missing implementation of specified behavior, contradictions between spec constraints and actual code. Output findings as a Markdown list. Each finding: one-line title, which AC/constraint it violates, and evidence from the diff.
26	
27	3. **Subagent failure handling**: If any subagent fails, times out, or returns empty results, append the layer name to `{failed_layers}` (comma-separated) and proceed with findings from the remaining layers.
28	
29	4. Collect all findings from the completed layers.
30	
31	
32	## NEXT
33	
34	Read fully and follow `./step-03-triage.md`
35

# Step 2: Review

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The Blind Hunter subagent receives NO project context — diff only.
- The Edge Case Hunter subagent receives diff and project read access.
- The Acceptance Auditor subagent receives diff, spec, and context docs.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`, note to the user: "Acceptance Auditor skipped — no spec file provided."

2. Launch parallel subagents without conversation context. If subagents are not available, generate prompt files in `{implementation_artifacts}` — one per reviewer role below — and HALT. Ask the user to run each in a separate session (ideally a different LLM) and paste back the findings. When findings are pasted, resume from this point and proceed to step 3.

   - **Blind Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` only. No spec, no context docs, no project access. Invoke via the `bmad-review-adversarial-general` skill.

   - **Edge Case Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` and read access to the project. Invoke via the `bmad-review-edge-case-hunter` skill.

   - **Acceptance Auditor** (only if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`) — receives `{diff_output}`, the content of the file at `{spec_file}`, and any loaded context docs. Its prompt:
     > You are an Acceptance Auditor. Review this diff against the spec and context docs. Check for: violations of acceptance criteria, deviations from spec intent, missing implementation of specified behavior, contradictions between spec constraints and actual code. Output findings as a Markdown list. Each finding: one-line title, which AC/constraint it violates, and evidence from the diff.

3. **Subagent failure handling**: If any subagent fails, times out, or returns empty results, append the layer name to `{failed_layers}` (comma-separated) and proceed with findings from the remaining layers.

4. Collect all findings from the completed layers.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-03-triage.md`

input.prompt:
You are a Blind Hunter code reviewer. You receive ONLY the diff — no spec, no project context, no codebase access. Your job is to find problems purely from what the diff itself shows.

Invoke the skill `bmad-review-adversarial-general` to structure your review. If that skill is not available, proceed anyway using its intent: a cynical, adversarial review that surfaces problems the author rationalized away.

The diff is at `/tmp/skf-story-1-3-diff.patch`. Read it with the Read tool. Do NOT read any other files in the repository — you are intentionally context-blind so you catch things a context-aware reviewer will miss.

Focus on:
- Internal inconsistencies WITHIN the diff (claim vs. content, section A vs. section B)
- Markdown/documentation quality: broken links, malformed tables, dead references, typos that change meaning
- Self-contradictions, ambiguous phrasing, instructions that won't survive a reader following them literally
- Commit message claims vs. diff reality
- Anything that reads as cargo-culted, hand-wavy, or aspirational rather than actionable

Output findings as a Markdown list. Each finding: one-line title, severity (critical/major/minor/nit), evidence quoted from the diff (with line context if possible). Do NOT invent findings to hit a quota — if the diff is clean in some area, say so.

Report under 800 words.

You are an Edge Case Hunter. Walk every branching path, boundary condition, and failure mode implied by this diff. Orthogonal to adversarial review — method-driven, not attitude-driven.

Invoke the skill `bmad-review-edge-case-hunter` to structure your review. If unavailable, proceed using its intent.

Inputs:
- Diff: `/tmp/skf-story-1-3-diff.patch` (Read tool)
- Full project read access. Project root: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge`
- Related files to consult as needed:
  - `docs/RELEASING.md` (target of diff — see post-change state)
  - `README.md` (target of diff)
  - `.github/workflows/` (does `release.yaml` exist yet? Story claims it doesn't)
  - `package.json` (for repository.url, publishConfig, etc.)
  - `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/epics.md` (Story 3.1 reference)

Context: This story registers an npm Trusted Publisher on npmjs.com (off-repo action) and updates two docs files. The trusted-publisher binding is: org=`armelhbobdad`, repo=`bmad-module-skill-forge`, workflow=`release.yaml`, environment=`release`. `release.yaml` does not yet exist — Story 3.1 will create it. This creates a "pre-registration inversion" where docs describe a binding to a workflow that doesn't exist.

Hunt for unhandled edge cases:
- What happens if Story 3.1 names the workflow `release.yml` instead of `release.yaml`? Does the doc explain how to recover?
- What if the `release` environment gets renamed? Downstream failure mode?
- Case sensitivity of org/repo/env/workflow fields — does the doc warn about any of it?
- If `NPM_TOKEN` is rotated or accidentally deleted before Story 6.3, what's the fallback?
- If a maintainer follows the RELEASING.md doc literally to verify/re-configure, are there missing steps or broken paths?
- README/RELEASING cross-references — do the anchors/links resolve?
- Does the commit actually produce the evidence AC #10 demands (timestamp, four field values, NPM_TOKEN confirmation)?
- Prettier normalization in the commit — are there any semantic changes masquerading as formatting?
- Any dead/orphaned markdown anchors after the reflow?

Output findings as a Markdown list. Each finding: one-line title, the specific edge case/boundary, evidence (file + line or diff hunk), and what fails. Only report UNHANDLED cases — if the diff addresses an edge case, skip it.

Report under 800 words.

<summary>Agent "Blind Hunter adversarial review" completed</summary>
<result>Now I have the full diff. Let me analyze it adversarially.

## Adversarial Review Findings — Story 1.3 diff (npm Trusted Publisher registration)

- **Commit title claims `release.yaml` but docs hedge on `.yaml` vs `.yml`** — major. Commit subject reads "register npm Trusted Publisher for release.yaml workflow" and the registered value is `release.yaml`, yet the Rename-coupling paragraph warns "flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml`" is load-bearing. The diff never establishes why `.yaml` was picked over GitHub's more common `.yml`; if Story 3.1 authors the workflow with `.yml` by habit, the first publish silently 404s. Lock the extension choice in the diff itself, not in a future story.

- **Self-contradiction on whether a publish was attempted** — major. Commit body says "No live publish attempted in this story: release.yaml does not yet exist." The "Fixing a bad registration" paragraph justifies delete-and-re-add with "there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2)". Fine — but the same diff also labels the token as "defence-in-depth fallback behind the OIDC path," implying an OIDC path exists. You cannot have a fallback behind a path that does not yet exist. One of the two framings is wrong.

- **"Silent 404 … with no actionable signal" asserted twice but never supported** — major. The section claims a mismatch "causes a silent `404` at publish time with no actionable signal," then in the next bullet quotes the message "npm could not match your workflow run." That is an actionable signal and it is not silent. Pick one: either the error is opaque (drop the quoted string) or it is diagnosable (drop "silent" and "no actionable signal"). Currently the doc contradicts itself across two adjacent paragraphs.

- **"First diagnostic … character-for-character" against a workflow that does not exist** — major. The Pre-registration inversion paragraph tells the reader, on a 404 in Story 3.2, to "verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header." But Story 3.1 writes `release.yaml`. If the workflow was authored wrong, the doc's instruction to treat the workflow as ground truth will have the reader "verify" a typo against itself. The correct first diagnostic is to compare both against the registered record in this RELEASING.md table — the doc misses that.

- **Commit body claims Prettier-only cosmetic drift; diff shows semantic-adjacent edits** — major. Body asserts "Semantic content of those pre-existing sections is unchanged." The `## Release Process` bullet in README changes from one-line prose to a four-clause enumeration naming the Trusted Publisher — that is a semantic change, not a prettier normalization. It belongs to this story, but the commit body buries it under "one-line extension" which understates the rewrite.

- **Italic-marker churn (`*…*` → `_…_`) is noise, not a required prettier behaviour** — minor. Prettier's default `--prose-wrap`/emphasis style does not rewrite `*foo*` to `_foo_` across a file; this is a configured preference. The commit frames it as mandatory ruleset compliance ("Prettier --write also normalized latent … drift"). Without a linked prettier config the reader cannot reproduce this and will wonder why the PR touches unrelated emphasis.

- **Environment name duplicated between ruleset and Trusted Publisher with no single source of truth** — major. `release` is hard-coded into the new table, into the README one-liner, and (per prior section) into the GitHub environment. The Rename-coupling paragraph says renaming requires a matching npm-side update but does not enumerate the README. Any future rename of the environment quietly leaves README.md out of sync.

- **Link label styled as code pointing at an external URL is a markdown antipattern** — minor. `[`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge)` renders a code-styled link whose visible text is the URL. Either use a descriptive label or drop the backticks; the current form is both redundant and style-inconsistent with the rest of the doc, which uses prose labels.

- **"Settings → Trusted Publisher section" UI claim is timestamped but not testable** — minor. "npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page" is a hedge, not a guarantee. npm has historically moved publisher settings between account-level and package-level. The doc promises a location it cannot promise.

- **`Entire-Checkpoint: e349ed56e39f` commit trailer is unexplained** — nit. No other commit in the shown history uses this trailer; its semantics are opaque to a reviewer. If it is meaningful to tooling, the convention should be documented; if not, it is noise in the permanent log.

- **"1 approver" + `prevent_self_review: false` reasoning is load-bearing but only linked sideways** — minor. The new section relies on the gate being meaningful ("required-reviewer gate"), but the adjacent existing section admits the solo maintainer self-approves. The Trusted Publisher section inherits that weakness without acknowledging it — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control.

- **SLSA Build Level 2 provenance claim is asserted, not cited** — nit. "auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation" appears in commit body and in the new section. npm's provenance feature's SLSA level is a moving target (was L2, advertised as L3 in some contexts depending on runner attestation). No link, no date-stamped citation. A blind reader cannot verify the level.

- **"Defence-in-depth" framing contradicts threat model** — major. NPM_TOKEN sitting at repo level is reachable by any workflow with `secrets.*` access on `main`; that is strictly more exposure than a scoped environment secret. Calling it "defence-in-depth fallback" inverts the security posture — a leftover token is an attack surface, not a safety net. The doc's own Story 6.3 acknowledges removal is the goal; the transitional label should be "legacy residual risk," not "defence-in-depth."

- **Rollback playbook placeholder left as bare HTML comment** — nit. `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` followed by a real `## Rollback Playbook` heading with no body. Readers hitting the TOC will click into an empty section. Either stub a "TBD — Story 4.1" line or omit the heading until it has content.

- **Clean areas** — the ruleset table reformat (pipe-alignment) and the Release Environment table reformat are mechanically consistent and introduce no semantic drift; the four registered fields match across commit body and the new markdown table exactly.</result>

- **Self-contradiction on whether a publish was attempted** — major. Commit body says "No live publish attempted in this story: release.yaml does not yet exist." The "Fixing a bad registration" paragraph justifies delete-and-re-add with "there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2)". Fine — but the same diff also labels the token as "defence-in-depth fallback behind the OIDC path," implying an OIDC path exists. You cannot have a fallback behind a path that does not yet exist. One of the two framings is wrong.

- **"Silent 404 … with no actionable signal" asserted twice but never supported** — major. The section claims a mismatch "causes a silent `404` at publish time with no actionable signal," then in the next bullet quotes the message "npm could not match your workflow run." That is an actionable signal and it is not silent. Pick one: either the error is opaque (drop the quoted string) or it is diagnosable (drop "silent" and "no actionable signal"). Currently the doc contradicts itself across two adjacent paragraphs.

- **"First diagnostic … character-for-character" against a workflow that does not exist** — major. The Pre-registration inversion paragraph tells the reader, on a 404 in Story 3.2, to "verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header." But Story 3.1 writes `release.yaml`. If the workflow was authored wrong, the doc's instruction to treat the workflow as ground truth will have the reader "verify" a typo against itself. The correct first diagnostic is to compare both against the registered record in this RELEASING.md table — the doc misses that.

- **Commit body claims Prettier-only cosmetic drift; diff shows semantic-adjacent edits** — major. Body asserts "Semantic content of those pre-existing sections is unchanged." The `## Release Process` bullet in README changes from one-line prose to a four-clause enumeration naming the Trusted Publisher — that is a semantic change, not a prettier normalization. It belongs to this story, but the commit body buries it under "one-line extension" which understates the rewrite.

- **Italic-marker churn (`*…*` → `_…_`) is noise, not a required prettier behaviour** — minor. Prettier's default `--prose-wrap`/emphasis style does not rewrite `*foo*` to `_foo_` across a file; this is a configured preference. The commit frames it as mandatory ruleset compliance ("Prettier --write also normalized latent … drift"). Without a linked prettier config the reader cannot reproduce this and will wonder why the PR touches unrelated emphasis.

- **Environment name duplicated between ruleset and Trusted Publisher with no single source of truth** — major. `release` is hard-coded into the new table, into the README one-liner, and (per prior section) into the GitHub environment. The Rename-coupling paragraph says renaming requires a matching npm-side update but does not enumerate the README. Any future rename of the environment quietly leaves README.md out of sync.

- **Link label styled as code pointing at an external URL is a markdown antipattern** — minor. `[`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge)` renders a code-styled link whose visible text is the URL. Either use a descriptive label or drop the backticks; the current form is both redundant and style-inconsistent with the rest of the doc, which uses prose labels.

- **"Settings → Trusted Publisher section" UI claim is timestamped but not testable** — minor. "npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page" is a hedge, not a guarantee. npm has historically moved publisher settings between account-level and package-level. The doc promises a location it cannot promise.

- **`Entire-Checkpoint: e349ed56e39f` commit trailer is unexplained** — nit. No other commit in the shown history uses this trailer; its semantics are opaque to a reviewer. If it is meaningful to tooling, the convention should be documented; if not, it is noise in the permanent log.

- **"1 approver" + `prevent_self_review: false` reasoning is load-bearing but only linked sideways** — minor. The new section relies on the gate being meaningful ("required-reviewer gate"), but the adjacent existing section admits the solo maintainer self-approves. The Trusted Publisher section inherits that weakness without acknowledging it — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control.

- **SLSA Build Level 2 provenance claim is asserted, not cited** — nit. "auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation" appears in commit body and in the new section. npm's provenance feature's SLSA level is a moving target (was L2, advertised as L3 in some contexts depending on runner attestation). No link, no date-stamped citation. A blind reader cannot verify the level.

- **"Defence-in-depth" framing contradicts threat model** — major. NPM_TOKEN sitting at repo level is reachable by any workflow with `secrets.*` access on `main`; that is strictly more exposure than a scoped environment secret. Calling it "defence-in-depth fallback" inverts the security posture — a leftover token is an attack surface, not a safety net. The doc's own Story 6.3 acknowledges removal is the goal; the transitional label should be "legacy residual risk," not "defence-in-depth."

- **Rollback playbook placeholder left as bare HTML comment** — nit. `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` followed by a real `## Rollback Playbook` heading with no body. Readers hitting the TOC will click into an empty section. Either stub a "TBD — Story 4.1" line or omit the heading until it has content.

- **Clean areas** — the ruleset table reformat (pipe-alignment) and the Release Environment table reformat are mechanically consistent and introduce no semantic drift; the four registered fields match across commit body and the new markdown table exactly.</result>

<result>I have sufficient evidence. Walking through the edge classes now.

Key findings from evidence:
- `release.yaml` does not exist in `.github/workflows/` yet (directory listing confirms only publish.yaml, manual-release.yaml, discord, docs, health-check, quality).
- `publish.yaml` already contains `npm publish --provenance` and uses `NPM_TOKEN` via `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` — it is NOT yet a trusted-publisher consumer but could accidentally try to use OIDC if invoked during transition.
- Epic AC #10 "evidence" was not supplied separately, but the commit body does claim timestamp, four fields, NPM_TOKEN status.
- Prettier reflow touched: README emphasis asterisks → underscores (4 occurrences), table pipe alignment in 3 tables. No semantic changes detected.
- README link `[docs/RELEASING.md](docs/RELEASING.md)` still resolves; anchor-free.
- The doc's "Inspect current state" step uses a URL click-through but offers no CLI/programmatic verification command (unlike every other section in RELEASING.md which does).
- The doc says environment-scoped secrets must be `0` (invariant) — if a maintainer reads the Release Environment section after this doc and accidentally scopes NPM_TOKEN there, nothing in the Trusted Publisher section cross-warns.

Now reporting findings:

# Edge Case Hunter Findings

- **Workflow filename extension drift handled in prose but not in evidence** — the doc warns about `.yaml` vs `.yml` rename coupling, but `.github/workflows/` currently contains zero files named `release.*`. Story 3.1's author has no machine-readable binding (no stub, no CODEOWNERS hint, no workflow comment) forcing the `.yaml` extension. Evidence: `ls .github/workflows/` shows only `discord.yaml, docs.yaml, health-check-dedup.yaml, manual-release.yaml, publish.yaml, quality.yaml`. **Fails if:** Story 3.1 author picks `.yml` (half of GitHub's own examples use `.yml`); the doc diagnoses the failure post-hoc, it does not prevent it.

- **No programmatic verification command for Trusted Publisher state** — every other section in `docs/RELEASING.md` offers a `gh api …` inspect command (ruleset § line 46, environment § line 91, branch policies § line 96, secrets § line 99). The new `## npm Trusted Publisher` section at line 169 gives only a UI click-path (`npmjs.com → Settings tab → Trusted Publisher`). **Fails if:** npm UI is reorganised, maintainer is offline-scripting a drift check, or an incident response needs machine-readable state. npm's `GET /-/package/bmad-module-skill-forge/access` / trusted-publisher list API is not referenced. No fallback.

- **`npm publish --provenance` in legacy `publish.yaml` will silently take the OIDC path once the Trusted Publisher is registered** — `publish.yaml:37` runs `npm publish --provenance` AND sets `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}`. Registering a Trusted Publisher makes npm's publish endpoint prefer OIDC when a valid `id-token` is present; `publish.yaml` already has `id-token: write` (line 10). The Trusted Publisher, however, binds workflow filename `release.yaml`, not `publish.yaml` — so any tag push firing `publish.yaml` now 404s on OIDC and falls through to token auth… or doesn't, depending on the exact npm policy precedence. The doc does not warn that registering the TP has non-local effects on the **existing** `publish.yaml` path. **Fails:** next `v*` tag-push before Story 3.3 retires `publish.yaml` may either 404 unexpectedly or publish without provenance, with no diagnostic anchored to this story.

- **Case sensitivity of the four fields is unstated** — GitHub organization and repository slugs are case-insensitive for routing but case-sensitive in OIDC subject claims; the npm TP matcher uses exact-string comparison. `docs/RELEASING.md:163-167` renders `armelhbobdad` / `bmad-module-skill-forge` / `release.yaml` / `release` lowercase but the surrounding prose never says "case-sensitive, do not capitalize on re-registration." **Fails if:** an admin re-registers using `armelhbobdad`'s display form (GitHub UI sometimes surfaces a capital-letter vanity), producing the exact "opaque 404" the doc warns about while appearing correct to the eye.

- **Environment rename/delete produces no observable signal before first publish** — `docs/RELEASING.md:87` in Release Environment says env rename requires npm-side update; the new § line 191 repeats this. Neither says what happens if the `release` GitHub environment is **deleted** (e.g. during an env-config refactor). Deletion without renaming leaves the npm TP pointing at a nonexistent env; no CI check, no status check, no pre-publish dry-run mentioned. **Fails:** first alpha cut (Story 3.2) 404s; nothing between Story 1.3 and Story 3.2 catches this.

- **NPM_TOKEN rotation/deletion before Story 6.3 has no documented fallback path** — § line 175 names `NPM_TOKEN` as "defence-in-depth fallback" and says "temporary flip of the new workflow to token-based publishing" is the last-resort escape. But it does not cover the inverse failure: if NPM_TOKEN is rotated, revoked, or accidentally deleted **while OIDC is also broken**, both paths are down. No "how to mint a short-lived token during incident" pointer, no link to npm token-creation docs. **Fails:** double-failure mode leaves release path inoperable with no escape hatch documented.

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

Update the story file Status section to `done`. - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. Save the story file. #### Sync sprint-status.yaml If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. #### Completion summary > **Review Complete!** > > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> > **Deferred:** <W> > **Dismissed:** <R> ### 7. Next steps Present the user with follow-up options: > **What would you like to do next?** > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again > 3. **Done** — end the workflow **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option.

content.content:
1	# Deferred Work
2	
3	Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.
4	
5	## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)
6	
7	- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.
8	
9	## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)
10	
11	- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
12	- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
13	- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
14	- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
15	- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
16	- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
17	- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

# Deferred Work

Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)

- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)

- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

279-- [x] [Review][Patch] README "Release Process" blurb omits the Release Environment / required-reviewer gate (AC #7 discoverability) [README.md:180] — current wording lists "branch-protection rules, required status checks, and (later) the rollback playbook"; Story 1.2's release-environment section is now the load-bearing piece and should be mentioned so maintainers can find it via the README
280-- [x] [Review][Patch] CODEOWNERS self-review claim misstates the GitHub mechanism [docs/RELEASING.md:39] — "GitHub disallows self-review as a code owner" conflates the general PR-author-cannot-self-approve rule with a code-owner-specific restriction. The deadlock for solo-maintainer + CODEOWNERS is real, but the mechanism is "author cannot approve their own PR" (applies to everyone). Rephrase for accuracy
281-- [x] [Review][Patch] Two-call restore is not idempotent — step 2 returns 422 on duplicate and the doc has no re-run guidance [docs/RELEASING.md:103-121] — `POST .../deployment-branch-policies` with `{"name": "main"}` errors if `main` is already in the allow-list. An incident responder copy-pasting the block a second time will see a confusing 422. Either note the expected 422-on-duplicate, or `DELETE` existing policies before the `POST`, or check first via `GET`

283-- [x] [Review][Defer] Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path [docs/RELEASING.md:14,69] — if either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s; defer a follow-up that swaps the id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). — deferred, cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook
284-- [x] [Review][Defer] "Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion [docs/RELEASING.md:77,82] — doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. — deferred, belongs with the release.yaml workflow (Story 3.1)
285-- [x] [Review][Defer] Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change [docs/RELEASING.md:26-34] — doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the two lists. — deferred, tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope
286-- [x] [Review][Defer] `prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory [docs/RELEASING.md:80] — no reminder anywhere that fires when a second reviewer is added. — deferred, low probability until solo-maintainer changes
287-- [x] [Review][Defer] `gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions [docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140] — a maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. — deferred, prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass
288-- [x] [Review][Defer] Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified [docs/RELEASING.md:52-65] — restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and a guidance on where baselines live. — deferred, Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home
289-- [x] [Review][Defer] Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified [docs/RELEASING.md:1-4] — frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. — deferred, confirm docs-site build config before acting
