input.old_string:
### Change Log

- 2026-04-20 — Registered npm Trusted Publisher for `bmad-module-skill-forge` (org=`armelhbobdad`, repo=`bmad-module-skill-forge`, workflow=`release.yaml`, env=`release`); added `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` and extended `README.md § Release Process` accordingly. `NPM_TOKEN` unchanged at repo scope.
input.new_string:
### Change Log

- 2026-04-20 — Registered npm Trusted Publisher for `bmad-module-skill-forge` (org=`armelhbobdad`, repo=`bmad-module-skill-forge`, workflow=`release.yaml`, env=`release`); added `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` and extended `README.md § Release Process` accordingly. `NPM_TOKEN` unchanged at repo scope.

### Review Findings

_Code review on 2026-04-20 against commit `bd20cda` on `feat/release-workflow`. Three adversarial layers: Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor. 2 decision-needed, 11 patch, 4 defer, 7 dismissed. Acceptance Auditor confirmed all 10 ACs are MET — remaining findings are quality-of-documentation and downstream-risk items, not AC violations._

- [ ] [Review][Decision] Workflow filename `.yaml` choice is prose-only, not enforced in repo — commit registers TP against `release.yaml` and the doc warns about the `.yaml`↔`.yml` coupling, but `.github/workflows/` has no artifact forcing Story 3.1 to pick `.yaml`. Options: (a) commit a stub `release.yaml` now, (b) add a README/CONTRIBUTING pin for the filename, (c) leave as prose-only and accept the failure mode is caught by Story 3.2's alpha cut.
- [ ] [Review][Decision] Legacy `publish.yaml` may interact with the new TP registration in unexpected ways — `publish.yaml:37` already runs `npm publish --provenance` with `id-token: write` (line 10). Once the TP is live, any `v*` tag push firing `publish.yaml` may be rejected by npm's OIDC preference (TP binds `release.yaml`, not `publish.yaml`) or silently fall back to token auth. Options: (a) add a "legacy publish path" warning to RELEASING.md, (b) disable `publish.yaml` triggers until Story 6.1 retires it, (c) accept the risk because no tags are expected before Story 3.x lands.

- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Defence-in-depth fallback behind the OIDC path" phrasing contradicts "release.yaml does not yet exist" [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher; commit body] — no OIDC path exists until Story 3.1; the token cannot be "behind" something that is not there. Rephrase as "legacy residual; scheduled removal in Story 6.3."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Silent 404 with no actionable signal" vs. quoted error string — self-contradiction [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — same section asserts the 404 is opaque and quotes the npm error message on the next line. Pick one: drop "silent/no actionable signal" or drop the quoted string.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] NPM_TOKEN framing as "defence-in-depth" inverts the threat model [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — a repo-scope token reachable from any workflow is an attack surface during the transition, not a safety net. Reframe as "legacy residual risk pending Story 6.3."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Pre-registration 404 diagnostic is circular — "verify fields against workflow header" [docs/RELEASING.md § Pre-registration inversion] — if `release.yaml` contains the typo, comparing TP fields against the workflow header confirms the typo. First diagnostic should be "compare both the workflow header AND npm's saved record against the table in this section."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Rename-coupling note omits README.md [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — section warns env/workflow-filename renames require an npm-side update but doesn't enumerate `README.md § Release Process` which also names `release` and the TP. Add README to the enumeration.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Code-styled URL link antipattern [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — ``[`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge)`` renders a code-styled link whose visible text equals the URL. Use a descriptive label (e.g. "npm package settings") or drop the backticks.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] No programmatic verification command for TP state [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — every other section in this doc offers a `gh api …` inspect command; TP section is UI-only. Either add a registry API call (npm exposes TP metadata on the package page JSON) or explicitly document the UI-only limitation with an in-line "No CLI/API inspection available as of 2026-04-20" note.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Case sensitivity of the four fields is unstated [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — npm's TP matcher is exact-string; GitHub sometimes surfaces capital-letter vanity for org slugs. Add one-line "Fields are case-sensitive; use the lowercase forms exactly as above."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Environment delete (vs. rename) has no observable signal path [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher; § Release Environment] — rename-coupling note covers renames; deletion of the `release` env leaves TP pointing at a nonexistent binding with no pre-publish warning. Extend rename-coupling to "rename OR delete."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Delete-and-re-add" safety claim is time-bounded but reads as evergreen [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — "no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2)" is only true until Story 3.2 lands. Post-3.2, a concurrent publish during the re-registration window will 404. Add caveat: "Pre-Story 3.2 only; post-3.2, gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] README → RELEASING.md link lands at top, not at the new section [README.md:180] — current phrasing "the npm Trusted Publisher registration" has no deep link. Swap to `[npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher)` using the GitHub auto-slug.

- [x] [Review][Defer] Solo-maintainer self-review weakness inherited from Story 1.2 [docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment] — new TP section relies on the "required reviewer" gate, which the adjacent Release Environment section admits is self-approved (`prevent_self_review: false`). The TP section does not re-acknowledge this. — deferred, Story 1.2's territory; already captured in Story 1.2 deferred list
- [x] [Review][Defer] Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content [docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook] — bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the placeholder comment. — deferred, Story 4.1 owns the rollback playbook content
- [x] [Review][Defer] `NPM_TOKEN` rotation/deletion before Story 6.3 has no documented fallback [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — section names the token as "last-resort token-based publish" but does not cover rotation/revocation during OIDC-broken windows. — deferred, belongs in Story 6.3's removal design
- [x] [Review][Defer] No CI/test asserts the TP registration's shape matches repo reality [docs/RELEASING.md; new CI check] — drift between npm-side TP and the `release.yaml`/`release` env it binds to is only caught at publish time (Story 3.2 onward). A CI check could pre-flight drift. — deferred, larger initiative; propose as standalone story once Story 3.x lands

+- [ ] [Review][Patch] README → RELEASING.md link lands at top, not at the new section [README.md:180] — current phrasing "the npm Trusted Publisher registration" has no deep link. Swap to `[npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher)` using the GitHub auto-slug.

# Deferred Work

Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)

- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)

- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

content.text:
### Code review complete.

**2** `decision-needed`, **11** `patch`, **4** `defer`, **7** dismissed as noise.

Findings written to the Review Findings section in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow.md`. Deferred items appended to `deferred-work.md`.

**Note:** Acceptance Auditor confirmed all 10 ACs are MET. Remaining findings are quality-of-documentation and downstream-risk items, not AC violations.

---

### Decision-needed findings — resolve these first

**D1. Workflow filename `.yaml` choice is prose-only, not enforced in repo.**

The commit registers the TP against `release.yaml`, and the doc warns about the `.yaml`↔`.yml` coupling being the #1 cause of opaque 404s. But `.github/workflows/` has no artifact right now forcing Story 3.1's author to pick `.yaml` over `.yml` — half of GitHub's own examples use `.yml`.

Options:
1. **Commit a stub `release.yaml`** — a minimal no-op workflow (or commented-out skeleton) that locks the filename now. Story 3.1 replaces the body.
2. **Add a README/CONTRIBUTING pin** — explicit "if you are Story 3.1: name the file `.github/workflows/release.yaml`, NOT `.yml`" callout somewhere a developer will see.
3. **Leave as prose-only** — accept that Story 3.2's alpha cut is the first live validator and the current doc's 404-diagnostic section is sufficient.
4. **Defer** — capture as a risk but don't patch now.
5. **Dismiss** — not worth the enforcement overhead.

Reply with the option number.

content.text:
**Option 4 — defer with a Story-3.1 handoff note.**

Here's the reasoning:

- **Option 1 (stub file)** is overkill. A permanent placeholder workflow in `.github/workflows/` creates real ongoing cost: it's visible on every CI run, maintainers will wonder what it's for, and if Story 3.1 slips it sits on main indefinitely. Heavy solution for a low-probability risk.
- **Option 3 (pure prose)** discards the edge-case hunter's concern with nothing to show for it. The warning lives in RELEASING.md which Story 3.1's author may or may not re-read before typing the filename.
- **Option 2 (README pin)** pollutes a reader-facing doc with a project-internal reminder — wrong audience.
- **Option 4 (defer)** is the zero-cost middle ground: you already have a `deferred-work.md` entry system where "check this before Story X lands" notes belong. When Story 3.1 is planned, that file will be read. The note becomes a Story-3.1 precondition instead of a Story-1.3 patch.

The risk profile also supports option 4: solo maintainer, same person wrote the warning, Story 3.1 spec already says `.yaml`, and Story 3.2's alpha cut is the designed safety net. Defense is three-deep already.

**Confirm option 4?** I'll add a Story-3.1 precondition bullet to `deferred-work.md`.

content.text:
D1 resolved — moved to defer.

---

### D2. Legacy `publish.yaml` may interact with the new TP registration in unexpected ways

`.github/workflows/publish.yaml` already has `id-token: write` (line 10) and runs `npm publish --provenance` with `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}` (line 37). Once the TP is live, any `v*` tag push that fires `publish.yaml` could:

- Attempt OIDC, get a 404 (TP binds `release.yaml`, not `publish.yaml`), fall back to token auth — with an opaque failure trace
- Or publish via token with provenance attached but without the TP chain — depending on npm's exact precedence rules

Story 6.1 retires `publish.yaml` post-v1.0.0. But between now and Story 6.1, a tag push could trigger unexpected behaviour.

Options:
1. **Warning paragraph in RELEASING.md** — add a "legacy publish path" note explaining what happens if `publish.yaml` fires post-TP-registration.
2. **Neutralize `publish.yaml`** — change its trigger to `workflow_dispatch` only (remove tag-push trigger) until Story 6.1 deletes it. Removes the accidental-fire surface.
3. **Accept the risk** — assume no tags will be pushed before Story 3.3 (when `release.yaml` merges and becomes canonical); rely on the solo maintainer not tagging by accident.
4. **Defer** — note the risk and leave for Story 3.3 or Story 6.1 to handle.
5. **Dismiss** — the interaction is speculative and npm's precedence behavior may not actually 404.

Reply with the option number.

content.text:
**Option 4 — defer as a Story-3.3 precondition.**

Reasoning:

- **Option 2 (neutralize `publish.yaml` now)** is the best _technical_ move but breaks Story 1.3's scope discipline. The story spec explicitly caps the envelope at "RELEASING.md + README.md only — no workflow YAML changes." A reviewer expanding that envelope is scope creep dressed as rigor.
- **The actual risk is smaller than the edge-case hunter framed it.** With `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` present, npm uses token auth regardless of TP state — the TP matcher is only consulted when no token is provided. A stray `v*` tag push would most likely produce a successful publish with "wrong" provenance attribution (claims `publish.yaml` published it, not `release.yaml`), not a hard 404. That's an audit-trail inconsistency, not a release outage.
- **Story 3.3's whole job is the release-path switch** — retiring `publish.yaml`'s tag trigger belongs there, atomically, alongside `release.yaml` becoming canonical. Splitting the neutralization off into a separate pre-3.3 change just creates two points of release-path state instead of one.
- **Option 1 (warning in RELEASING.md)** documents a risk we're about to retire; the prose will be stale in one story's time.

So: defer, with an explicit Story-3.3 precondition saying "when merging `release.yaml` to main, simultaneously remove the `v*` tag trigger from `publish.yaml` (or gate it behind `workflow_dispatch` only) so the release path is single-rooted."

**Confirm option 4?** I'll add the Story-3.3 precondition to `deferred-work.md`.

# Deferred Work

Items pulled out of review/implementation to preserve scope, tracked here so they don't get lost.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-1-audit-and-harden-main-branch-protection (2026-04-20)

- **Restore snippet covers ruleset-edit recovery but not ruleset-deletion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:74-80` shows a `PUT` against an existing ruleset id. If the ruleset is deleted, the restore requires `POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` with the full ruleset body (including `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions`). Story 4.1 owns the full rollback playbook and is the natural home for this expansion.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer (2026-04-20)

- **Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path** — `docs/RELEASING.md:14,69`. If either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s. Swap id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). Cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook.
- **"Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion** — `docs/RELEASING.md:77,82`. Doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. Belongs with the `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1).
- **Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change** — `docs/RELEASING.md:26-34`. Doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the ruleset's check list against the workflow's job/matrix keys. Tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope.
- **`prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory** — `docs/RELEASING.md:80`. No reminder fires when a second reviewer is added. Low probability until solo-maintainer changes; revisit with team-growth.
- **`gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions** — `docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140`. A maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. Prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass.
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow (2026-04-20)

The TP section does not re-acknowledge this — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control. Belongs with the Story 1.2 self-review gap (already captured there).
- **Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook`. Bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder. Readers hitting the TOC click into an empty section until Story 4.1 lands. Story 4.1's natural territory.

Section names the token as "last-resort token-based publish" if OIDC breaks, but does not cover the inverse failure: token rotated/revoked during an OIDC-broken window leaves both paths down. Standalone story once Story 3.x lands.

150	  "repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/$POLICY_ID"
151	```
152	
153	Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.
154	
155	## npm Trusted Publisher
156	
157	The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trusted publishing** — no `NPM_TOKEN` is consulted during the publish step, and every published version carries an auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation. For this to work, the npm package `bmad-module-skill-forge` has a trusted-publisher entry on npmjs.com that binds on four fields exactly matching what the workflow asserts at run time. A mismatch on any field causes a silent `404` at publish time with no actionable signal.
158	
159	**Registered:** 2026-04-20 by `armelhbobdad`.
160	
161	| Field             | Value                     |
162	| ----------------- | ------------------------- |
163	| Publisher type    | `GitHub Actions`          |
164	| Organization/user | `armelhbobdad`            |
165	| Repository        | `bmad-module-skill-forge` |
166	| Workflow filename | `release.yaml`            |
167	| Environment       | `release`                 |
168	
169	**Inspect current state:** visit [`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account.
170	
171	**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require a matching update to the npm-side Trusted Publisher in the same change. Skipping the npm-side update produces an opaque `404` ("npm could not match your workflow run") on the next publish with no actionable signal.
172	
173	**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, the **first** diagnostic is to reload the npm Settings tab and verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header; the workflow YAML is the second-line check, not the first.
174	
175	**`NPM_TOKEN` is still live during the transition.** The legacy `publish.yaml` continues to use `NPM_TOKEN` until Story 3.3 retires it. Story 6.3 removes the `NPM_TOKEN` secret from repo settings entirely post-v1.0.0. Until then, the token is defence-in-depth: if OIDC fails unexpectedly, the fallback path is a temporary flip of the new workflow to token-based publishing (last-resort; document the reason in the commit body if ever exercised).
176	
177	**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous — there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2), and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner.
178	
179	<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->
180	
181	## Rollback Playbook
182	
183	_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._
184

```

Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.

## npm Trusted Publisher

The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trusted publishing** — no `NPM_TOKEN` is consulted during the publish step, and every published version carries an auto-attached SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation. For this to work, the npm package `bmad-module-skill-forge` has a trusted-publisher entry on npmjs.com that binds on four fields exactly matching what the workflow asserts at run time. A mismatch on any field causes a silent `404` at publish time with no actionable signal.

**Registered:** 2026-04-20 by `armelhbobdad`.

| Field             | Value                     |
| ----------------- | ------------------------- |
| Publisher type    | `GitHub Actions`          |
| Organization/user | `armelhbobdad`            |
| Repository        | `bmad-module-skill-forge` |
| Workflow filename | `release.yaml`            |
| Environment       | `release`                 |

**Inspect current state:** visit [`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account.

**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require a matching update to the npm-side Trusted Publisher in the same change. Skipping the npm-side update produces an opaque `404` ("npm could not match your workflow run") on the next publish with no actionable signal.

**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, the **first** diagnostic is to reload the npm Settings tab and verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header; the workflow YAML is the second-line check, not the first.

**`NPM_TOKEN` is still live during the transition.** The legacy `publish.yaml` continues to use `NPM_TOKEN` until Story 3.3 retires it. Story 6.3 removes the `NPM_TOKEN` secret from repo settings entirely post-v1.0.0. Until then, the token is defence-in-depth: if OIDC fails unexpectedly, the fallback path is a temporary flip of the new workflow to token-based publishing (last-resort; document the reason in the commit body if ever exercised).

**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous — there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2), and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner.

<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->

## Rollback Playbook

_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._

lines: **Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming OR deleting the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require matching updates in the same PR to: (a) the npm-side Trusted Publisher, (b) the Registered table above, and (c) the `## Release Process` enumeration in `README.md` (which names both `release` and the Trusted Publisher). Skipping any of these produces an opaque `404` on the next publish — the error names the failure class, not the specific field.

-**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, the **first** diagnostic is to reload the npm Settings tab and verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header; the workflow YAML is the second-line check, not the first.

+**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, open a **three-way comparison**: (1) the npm Settings tab, (2) the workflow YAML's `name` / `on` / `jobs.<id>.environment` lines, and (3) the Registered table above. The table is the ground truth because it captured the values at npm-save time — compare both the npm record and the workflow header against the table, never the workflow against itself (verifying the workflow against its own header will silently confirm a typo).

**`NPM_TOKEN` is still live during the transition.** The legacy `publish.yaml` continues to use `NPM_TOKEN` until Story 3.3 retires it. Story 6.3 removes the `NPM_TOKEN` secret from repo settings entirely post-v1.0.0. Until then, the token is defence-in-depth: if OIDC fails unexpectedly, the fallback path is a temporary flip of the new workflow to token-based publishing (last-resort; document the reason in the commit body if ever exercised).

input.old_string: **`NPM_TOKEN` is still live during the transition.** The legacy `publish.yaml` continues to use `NPM_TOKEN` until Story 3.3 retires it. Story 6.3 removes the `NPM_TOKEN` secret from repo settings entirely post-v1.0.0. input.new_string: **`NPM_TOKEN` is a legacy residual, not a safety net.** The token remains at repo-level secrets for two reasons: (a) the legacy `publish.yaml` still uses it until Story 3.3 retires that path, and (b) Story 6.3 deletes the secret entirely post-v1.0.0. Treat its continued presence as attack surface to minimize, not defence-in-depth — a repo-scope token is reachable from any workflow with `secrets.*` access. The token does NOT sit "behind" OIDC: `release.yaml` does not yet exist, so there is no OIDC path for it to be a fallback to. If a future OIDC incident forces a last-resort token-based re-publish, document the flip in the commit body and revert as soon as OIDC is restored.

**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous — there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2), and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner.

content.content:
241:- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Defence-in-depth fallback behind the OIDC path" phrasing contradicts "release.yaml does not yet exist" [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher; commit body] — no OIDC path exists until Story 3.1; the token cannot be "behind" something that is not there. Rephrase as "legacy residual; scheduled removal in Story 6.3."
242:- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Silent 404 with no actionable signal" vs. quoted error string — self-contradiction [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — same section asserts the 404 is opaque and quotes the npm error message on the next line. Pick one: drop "silent/no actionable signal" or drop the quoted string.
243:- [ ] [Review][Patch] NPM_TOKEN framing as "defence-in-depth" inverts the threat model [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — a repo-scope token reachable from any workflow is an attack surface during the transition, not a safety net. Reframe as "legacy residual risk pending Story 6.3."
244:- [ ] [Review][Patch] Pre-registration 404 diagnostic is circular — "verify fields against workflow header" [docs/RELEASING.md § Pre-registration inversion] — if `release.yaml` contains the typo, comparing TP fields against the workflow header confirms the typo. First diagnostic should be "compare both the workflow header AND npm's saved record against the table in this section."
245:- [ ] [Review][Patch] Rename-coupling note omits README.md [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — section warns env/workflow-filename renames require an npm-side update but doesn't enumerate `README.md § Release Process` which also names `release` and the TP. Add README to the enumeration.
246:- [ ] [Review][Patch] Code-styled URL link antipattern [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — ``[`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge)`` renders a code-styled link whose visible text equals the URL. Use a descriptive label (e.g. "npm package settings") or drop the backticks.
247:- [ ] [Review][Patch] No programmatic verification command for TP state [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — every other section in this doc offers a `gh api …` inspect command; TP section is UI-only. Either add a registry API call (npm exposes TP metadata on the package page JSON) or explicitly document the UI-only limitation with an in-line "No CLI/API inspection available as of 2026-04-20" note.
248:- [ ] [Review][Patch] Case sensitivity of the four fields is unstated [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — npm's TP matcher is exact-string; GitHub sometimes surfaces capital-letter vanity for org slugs. Add one-line "Fields are case-sensitive; use the lowercase forms exactly as above."
249:- [ ] [Review][Patch] Environment delete (vs. rename) has no observable signal path [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher; § Release Environment] — rename-coupling note covers renames; deletion of the `release` env leaves TP pointing at a nonexistent binding with no pre-publish warning. Extend rename-coupling to "rename OR delete."
250:- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Delete-and-re-add" safety claim is time-bounded but reads as evergreen [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — "no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2)" is only true until Story 3.2 lands. Post-3.2, a concurrent publish during the re-registration window will 404. Add caveat: "Pre-Story 3.2 only; post-3.2, gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze."
251:- [ ] [Review][Patch] README → RELEASING.md link lands at top, not at the new section [README.md:180] — current phrasing "the npm Trusted Publisher registration" has no deep link. Swap to `[npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher)` using the GitHub auto-slug.

+- [x] [Review][Patch] "Silent 404 with no actionable signal" vs. quoted error string — self-contradiction [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — same section asserts the 404 is opaque and quotes the npm error message on the next line. Pick one: drop "silent/no actionable signal" or drop the quoted string.

+- [x] [Review][Patch] NPM_TOKEN framing as "defence-in-depth" inverts the threat model [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — a repo-scope token reachable from any workflow is an attack surface during the transition, not a safety net. Reframe as "legacy residual risk pending Story 6.3."

+- [x] [Review][Patch] Pre-registration 404 diagnostic is circular — "verify fields against workflow header" [docs/RELEASING.md § Pre-registration inversion] — if `release.yaml` contains the typo, comparing TP fields against the workflow header confirms the typo. First diagnostic should be "compare both the workflow header AND npm's saved record against the table in this section."

+- [x] [Review][Patch] Rename-coupling note omits README.md [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — section warns env/workflow-filename renames require an npm-side update but doesn't enumerate `README.md § Release Process` which also names `release` and the TP. Add README to the enumeration.

+- [x] [Review][Patch] Code-styled URL link antipattern [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — ``[`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge)`` renders a code-styled link whose visible text equals the URL. Use a descriptive label (e.g. "npm package settings") or drop the backticks.

+- [x] [Review][Patch] No programmatic verification command for TP state [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — every other section in this doc offers a `gh api …` inspect command; TP section is UI-only. Either add a registry API call (npm exposes TP metadata on the package page JSON) or explicitly document the UI-only limitation with an in-line "No CLI/API inspection available as of 2026-04-20" note.

+- [x] [Review][Patch] Case sensitivity of the four fields is unstated [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — npm's TP matcher is exact-string; GitHub sometimes surfaces capital-letter vanity for org slugs. Add one-line "Fields are case-sensitive; use the lowercase forms exactly as above."

+- [x] [Review][Patch] Environment delete (vs. rename) has no observable signal path [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher; § Release Environment] — rename-coupling note covers renames; deletion of the `release` env leaves TP pointing at a nonexistent binding with no pre-publish warning. Extend rename-coupling to "rename OR delete."

+- [x] [Review][Patch] "Delete-and-re-add" safety claim is time-bounded but reads as evergreen [docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher] — "no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2)" is only true until Story 3.2 lands. Post-3.2, a concurrent publish during the re-registration window will 404. Add caveat: "Pre-Story 3.2 only; post-3.2, gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze."

+- [x] [Review][Patch] README → RELEASING.md link lands at top, not at the new section [README.md:180] — current phrasing "the npm Trusted Publisher registration" has no deep link. Swap to `[npm Trusted Publisher registration](docs/RELEASING.md#npm-trusted-publisher)` using the GitHub auto-slug.

- [x] [Review][Defer] Solo-maintainer self-review weakness inherited from Story 1.2 [docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment] — new TP section relies on the "required reviewer" gate, which the adjacent Release Environment section admits is self-approved (`prevent_self_review: false`). The TP section does not re-acknowledge this. — deferred, Story 1.2's territory; already captured in Story 1.2 deferred list

- [x] [Review][Defer] Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content [docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook] — bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the placeholder comment. — deferred, Story 4.1 owns the rollback playbook content

@@ -166,15 +166,17 @@ The future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) publishes to npm via **OIDC trust
 | Workflow filename | `release.yaml`            |
 | Environment       | `release`                 |
 
-**Inspect current state:** visit [`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account.
+**Inspect current state:** visit the [npm package settings for `bmad-module-skill-forge`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) → **Settings** tab → **Trusted Publisher** section (npm UI as of 2026-04-20; if the tab is reorganised later, the section still lives on the package Settings page). Modification requires 2FA re-entry on the maintainer account. No CLI or public API for programmatic inspection of Trusted Publisher state exists as of 2026-04-20 — drift detection is UI-only until npm exposes one.
 
-**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require a matching update to the npm-side Trusted Publisher in the same change. Skipping the npm-side update produces an opaque `404` ("npm could not match your workflow run") on the next publish with no actionable signal.
+**Case-sensitive.** All four fields above use the exact lowercase forms shown; npm's matcher is an exact-string comparison. Do not capitalize on re-registration even if GitHub's UI surfaces a display-form with capitals.
 
-**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, the **first** diagnostic is to reload the npm Settings tab and verify all four fields character-for-character against the workflow's header; the workflow YAML is the second-line check, not the first.
+**Rename coupling — the four fields are load-bearing.** Renaming OR deleting the `release` GitHub environment (see § Release Environment), renaming or moving `release.yaml` within `.github/workflows/`, or flipping the extension between `.yaml` and `.yml` each require matching updates in the same PR to: (a) the npm-side Trusted Publisher, (b) the Registered table above, and (c) the `## Release Process` enumeration in `README.md` (which names both `release` and the Trusted Publisher). Skipping any of these produces an opaque `404` on the next publish — the error names the failure class, not the specific field.
 
-**`NPM_TOKEN` is still live during the transition.** The legacy `publish.yaml` continues to use `NPM_TOKEN` until Story 3.3 retires it. Story 6.3 removes the `NPM_TOKEN` secret from repo settings entirely post-v1.0.0. Until then, the token is defence-in-depth: if OIDC fails unexpectedly, the fallback path is a temporary flip of the new workflow to token-based publishing (last-resort; document the reason in the commit body if ever exercised).
+**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, open a **three-way comparison**: (1) the npm Settings tab, (2) the workflow YAML's `name` / `on` / `jobs.<id>.environment` lines, and (3) the Registered table above. The table is the ground truth because it captured the values at npm-save time — compare both the npm record and the workflow header against the table, never the workflow against itself (verifying the workflow against its own header will silently confirm a typo).
 
-**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous — there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2), and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner.
+**`NPM_TOKEN` is a legacy residual, not a safety net.** The token remains at repo-level secrets for two reasons: (a) the legacy `publish.yaml` still uses it until Story 3.3 retires that path, and (b) Story 6.3 deletes the secret entirely post-v1.0.0. Treat its continued presence as attack surface to minimize, not defence-in-depth — a repo-scope token is reachable from any workflow with `secrets.*` access. The token does NOT sit "behind" OIDC: `release.yaml` does not yet exist, so there is no OIDC path for it to be a fallback to. If a future OIDC incident forces a last-resort token-based re-publish, document the flip in the commit body and revert as soon as OIDC is restored.
+
+**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous. **Pre-Story 3.2**: there is no destructive side effect because no publish is attempted yet, and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner. **Post-Story 3.2**: a publish that fires during the delete-and-re-add window will 404 — gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze (pause any active `release.yaml` runs, confirm no tags are in-flight) before touching the entry.

+**Pre-registration inversion.** This entry was registered **before** `release.yaml` was authored (Story 3.1). The first live validator of the full OIDC chain is Story 3.2's alpha cut. If that cut's publish step 404s, open a **three-way comparison**: (1) the npm Settings tab, (2) the workflow YAML's `name` / `on` / `jobs.<id>.environment` lines, and (3) the Registered table above. The table is the ground truth because it captured the values at npm-save time — compare both the npm record and the workflow header against the table, never the workflow against itself (verifying the workflow against its own header will silently confirm a typo).
 
-**Fixing a bad registration.** The npm UI exposes both **Edit** and **Delete** on an existing Trusted Publisher entry (observed 2026-04-20). Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous — there is no destructive side effect (no publish is attempted until Story 3.2), and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner.
+**`NPM_TOKEN` is a legacy residual, not a safety net.** The token remains at repo-level secrets for two reasons: (a) the legacy `publish.yaml` still uses it until Story 3.3 retires that path, and (b) Story 6.3 deletes the secret entirely post-v1.0.0. Treat its continued presence as attack surface to minimize, not defence-in-depth — a repo-scope token is reachable from any workflow with `secrets.*` access. The token does NOT sit "behind" OIDC: `release.yaml` does not yet exist, so there is no OIDC path for it to be a fallback to. If a future OIDC incident forces a last-resort token-based re-publish, document the flip in the commit body and revert as soon as OIDC is restored.
+

Prefer edit for a single-field typo; prefer delete-and-re-add if multiple fields are wrong or the edit form ever feels ambiguous. **Pre-Story 3.2**: there is no destructive side effect because no publish is attempted yet, and delete-and-re-add keeps the audit trail cleaner. **Post-Story 3.2**: a publish that fires during the delete-and-re-add window will 404 — gate any delete-and-re-add behind a manual publish freeze (pause any active `release.yaml` runs, confirm no tags are in-flight) before touching the entry.

content.text: `_bmad-output/` is gitignored — only `README.md` and `docs/RELEASING.md` are stageable. Matches the pattern of prior commit `dec627d docs(releasing): apply code-review patches to hardened release docs`.

input.command:
git add README.md docs/RELEASING.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(releasing): apply code-review patches to npm Trusted Publisher docs

Applies 11 patches from Story 1.3 code review (adversarial layers:
Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor). All 10 Story 1.3
acceptance criteria were MET before these patches — changes are
quality-of-documentation and downstream-risk hardening, not AC fixes.

docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher:

- "silent 404 / no actionable signal" claim replaced with "opaque
  404" + the actual npm error string ("npm could not match your
  workflow run"). The old text self-contradicted — asserting opacity
  while quoting the observable message on the next line.
- "NPM_TOKEN is defence-in-depth" reframed as "NPM_TOKEN is a legacy
  residual, not a safety net." A repo-scope token reachable from any
  workflow with secrets.* access is attack surface to minimize during
  the transition, not a fallback. The old framing also claimed the
  token sat "behind the OIDC path" when no OIDC path exists yet —
  release.yaml is not authored until Story 3.1.
- Pre-registration 404 diagnostic rewritten as a three-way comparison
  (npm Settings tab / workflow YAML header / Registered table), with
  the table as ground truth. Old text told readers to verify the
  workflow header "character-for-character" — circular when the
  workflow itself carries the typo.
- Rename-coupling now enumerates three load-bearing locations that
  must move together in the same PR: the npm-side TP, the Registered
  table, and the README § Release Process enumeration. Previously
  README was unlisted, so renames could silently drift it. Coverage
  extended to include environment DELETE, not just rename.
- Delete-and-re-add safety claim split into a pre-Story-3.2 mode (no
  publish in flight, safe to delete freely) and a post-Story-3.2 mode
  (gate behind a manual publish freeze — pause active release.yaml
  runs, confirm no tags in-flight). The old unconditional "no
  destructive side effect" would read as evergreen after Story 3.2.
- Inspection URL relinked with a descriptive label ("npm package
  settings for bmad-module-skill-forge"). Old markup was
  [`url`](url) — code-styled visible text equalling the URL itself.
- Added limitation note: no CLI or public API for programmatic
  Trusted Publisher inspection exists as of 2026-04-20; drift
  detection is UI-only until npm exposes one.
- Added case-sensitivity note: the four fields use exact-string
  matching; do not capitalize on re-registration even if GitHub's UI
  surfaces a display-form with capitals.

README.md § Release Process:

- Deep-linked the `release` environment and npm Trusted Publisher
  references to #release-environment and #npm-trusted-publisher
  anchors in docs/RELEASING.md. A reader clicking "the npm Trusted
  Publisher registration" previously landed at the top of the file.

Two decision-needed review items routed to defer as future-story
preconditions (Story 3.1 workflow-filename enforcement; Story 3.3
atomic retirement of publish.yaml's tag-push trigger). Both are
captured in the local maintainer tracking artifact and do not block
this branch.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git status
